name: general
description: Universal software engineering practices applicable to any language or stack

decisions.decision: Use Conventional Commits format with imperative mood
decisions.rationale: Conventional Commits enable automated versioning (semver), changelog generation, and provide a parseable commit history. Imperative mood ('add feature' not 'added feature') matches git's own conventions (merge, revert).
decisions.details: Format: <type>[scope]: <description>. Types: feat (MINOR), fix (PATCH), docs, test, chore, refactor, perf, ci, build. Append ! for MAJOR: feat!: remove deprecated endpoint. Body explains WHY, not WHAT. Footer: Closes #123, BREAKING CHANGE: description.

decisions.decision: Small, focused pull requests with squash merge to main
decisions.rationale: Small PRs are easier to review, less likely to introduce bugs, and faster to merge. Squash merge keeps main's history clean and parseable for automated tooling.
decisions.details: One concern per PR (feature, fix, or refactor). Title <70 chars; description covers motivation + test plan. Never mix refactoring with feature work. Split large changes into stacked PRs with clear dependencies.

decisions.decision: Pin dependencies, automate updates, audit vulnerabilities in CI
decisions.rationale: Unpinned dependencies lead to non-reproducible builds and supply chain risk. Automated update PRs (Dependabot, Renovate) keep dependencies current without manual toil.
decisions.details: Commit lockfiles (go.sum, package-lock.json, Cargo.lock). Enable Dependabot or Renovate. Run vuln scanning in CI: govulncheck (Go), cargo audit (Rust), npm audit (Node). Prefer narrow, well-maintained libs over kitchen-sink frameworks.

decisions.decision: Never commit secrets; use environment-specific injection
decisions.rationale: Committed secrets are near-impossible to fully remove from git history and are a common attack vector. Environment injection ensures secrets differ per environment and are managed by infrastructure.
decisions.details: .env files locally (always in .gitignore). Use GitHub Actions secrets, Vault, or cloud KMS in CI/CD. Rotate regularly. Run secret detection in CI: gitleaks, trufflehog, or aide secrets analyzer. If committed, rotate immediately — never rely on history rewriting alone.

decisions.decision: Store learnings, gotchas, patterns, and session context via aide memory, not local assistant memory
decisions.rationale: Local assistant memory (e.g. Claude Code MEMORY.md, OpenCode memory) is scoped to a single assistant and invisible to other agents, swarm workers, or different AI tools working on the same project. aide memories are shared across all sessions, agents, and assistant implementations, and are injected at session start with scoring and decay.
decisions.details: Use `aide memory add --category=<cat> --tags=<tags> "<content>"` (or the aide MCP memory tools) to persist learnings, patterns, gotchas, abandoned approaches, and session summaries. Categories: learning, pattern, gotcha, abandoned, session. Always include provenance tags (source:user|discovered|inferred, verified:true|false for codebase claims). Memories are soft facts — useful context that may become stale. Do not over-rely on them; verify against current code before acting on recalled memories. Prefer not storing over storing something unverified. Never use the assistant's native/local memory system for project knowledge.

decisions.decision: Record architectural and technical decisions via aide decisions, not local assistant memory
decisions.rationale: Decisions are project guardrails that constrain all future work. Storing them in local assistant memory makes them invisible to other agents, swarm workers, and different AI tools. aide decisions are injected into every session context, respected by all agents, and maintain supersession history.
decisions.details: Use `aide decision set "<topic-slug>" "<summary>" --rationale="<why>"` (or the aide MCP decision tools) to record significant technical choices. Decisions are hard rules — they supersede defaults and act as guardrails. Good candidates: technology choices, architectural patterns, testing strategies, API design, deployment approaches. Not needed for: minor implementation details, personal preferences (use memories), or temporary experiments. Decisions can be superseded by re-running the decide workflow for the same topic (history is preserved). Never store architectural decisions in the assistant's native/local memory system.
